:apcswt_model_status:                    <<apcswt_model_status,Model Status>>
:apcswt_normalized_text:                 <<apcswt_normalized_text,Wire Normalized Text>>
:apcswt_transaction_index:               <<apcswt_transaction_index,Transaction Index>>

// end::mapping-section[]


== Overview

This document defines the formal vocabulary for the detection pipeline in Ann's PHI Clipbuddy.
It specifies the entities the algorithm operates on, the resources it consults, the procedures it performs, and the execution sites at which each discerner runs.

The application specification (APCAS) owns the user experience.
The prototype specification (APCPS) owns implementation details, tech stack, data sources, wire-format JSON schemas, and the container image contents.
This document owns the detection pipeline's conceptual structure — the terms that must be precise for the pipeline to be reasoned about correctly.

The pipeline has three named procedures with ten parallel discerners between the first and second.
{apcsgn_normalization}
produces the canonical input.
Ten discerners each run as a pure function of
{apcsgt_normalized_text},
producing their own intimately-named finding type;
seven of them run inside the Rust application and three of them run inside
{apcscc_container}.
{apcsnc_combining}
fuses the ten finding streams into
{apcsnu_unified_finding_s}
covering regions of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}.
{apcsna_anonymization}
produces
{apcsnt_anonymized_text}.

Byte offsets, character arithmetic, and tokenizer mechanics live in code.
This vocabulary talks about regions of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
— the addressable units of detection — and leaves their concrete representation as an implementation concern.


== Input

[[apcsuc_clipboard_content]]
{apcsuc_clipboard_content}::
Raw content read from the system clipboard on window focus.
May be HTML or plain text.
Consumed once, then the system clipboard is cleared.

[[apcsue_epic_html]]
{apcsue_epic_html}::
HTML produced by Epic's "Copy All" function.
Semi-structured: bold labels for demographics, section headers for clinical categories, narrative freetext for clinical prose, signature blocks for provider attribution.
The structural formatting is the primary advantage over plain text — labels anchor PHI detection with high precision.

[[apcsug_clinical_gate]]
{apcsug_clinical_gate}::
A heuristic that decides whether
{apcsuc_clipboard_content}
is clinical.
Scans for known label patterns (Patient:, DOB:, MRN:, etc.).
Acceptance threshold: two or more recognized labels present.
Content that fails the
{apcsug_clinical_gate}
is not processed; the triage view clears.


== Parsed Structure

The parsed structure captures the semantic regions Epic's HTML encodes — labeled demographic fields, section headers, narrative prose.
Parsing is an implementation-level step of
{apcsgn_normalization};
this vocabulary exists because the narrative discussion of individual discerners refers to structural categories when explaining how they decide.
Region membership belongs to the concept model; byte offsets and string arithmetic do not.

[[apcsdo_document]]
{apcsdo_document}::
The result of parsing
{apcsue_epic_html}.
A sequence of typed
{apcsgs_span_s}
whose concatenated text contributions form
{apcsgt_normalized_text}.

[[apcsgs_span]]
{apcsgs_span}::
A typed region within the
{apcsdo_document}.
Each
{apcsgs_span}
has a kind, an optional label, and text content.
Three kinds:
{apcsdl_labeled_field},
{apcsdh_section_header},
{apcsdn_narrative}.

[[apcsdl_labeled_field]]
{apcsdl_labeled_field}::
A
{apcsgs_span}
produced by a bold label followed by a value (e.g., `Patient: Margaret J. Thornton`).
The label is the bold text before the colon.
The value is the text after the colon, extending to the next structural boundary (another label, line break, or HTML element boundary).

[[apcsdh_section_header]]
{apcsdh_section_header}::
A
{apcsgs_span}
produced by a bold label matching a known clinical section name (Chief Complaint, History of Present Illness, Assessment/Plan, etc.).
Contributes the header text to
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
but carries no value — the section body follows as subsequent
{apcsgs_span_s}.

[[apcsdn_narrative]]
{apcsdn_narrative}::
A
{apcsgs_span}
containing freetext not attached to a label.
Clinical prose, family history details, plan items.
PHI in
{apcsdn_narrative}
{apcsgs_span_s}
lacks positional anchoring and must be detected by the discerners that do not depend on Epic labels.


== Detection Resources

=== Registries

[[apcsdt_label_registry]]
{apcsdt_label_registry}::
The enumerated set of
{apcsdl_labeled_field}
labels that trigger per-word PHI extraction in the
{apcsds_label}.
Each entry specifies the label text (case-insensitive match) and the
{apcsgc_phi_category}
assigned to extracted words.
+
[cols="2,1"]
|===
| Label | Category

| Patient:           | NAME
| Attending:         | PROVIDER
| Provider:          | PROVIDER
| Referring:         | PROVIDER
| Facility:          | FACILITY
| Electronically signed by: | PROVIDER
| Emergency Contact  | NAME
| Primary Care Physician | PROVIDER
|===

[[apcsdt_regex_table]]
{apcsdt_regex_table}::
The enumerated set of regular expression patterns that detect structurally identifiable PHI in the
{apcsds_regex}.
Each entry specifies a pattern, a
{apcsgc_phi_category},
and a capture group index.
See APCPS for the full pattern table.

=== Dictionaries

[[apcsdd_surname]]
{apcsdd_surname}::
US Census 2010 surnames, top 1,000 by frequency.
Source: public domain.
Consumed by the
{apcsds_surname}.

[[apcsdd_firstname]]
{apcsdd_firstname}::
SSA baby names, top 1,000 by aggregate frequency.
Source: public domain.
Consumed by the
{apcsds_firstname}.

[[apcsdd_city]]
{apcsdd_city}::
US state capitals plus top 100 cities by population.
Source: public domain.
Consumed by the
{apcsds_city}.

[[apcsdw_english]]
{apcsdw_english}::
Top 5,000 common English words (Norvig/Google n-grams).
Source: public domain.
Consumed by the
{apcsds_english}.

[[apcsdw_medical]]
{apcsdw_medical}::
Hand-curated medications, lab tests, diagnoses, anatomy, abbreviations.
Source: original curation.
Consumed by the
{apcsds_medical}.

NOTE: There is no umbrella "blacklist" or "whitelist" concept in this vocabulary.
Earlier drafts grouped the three name/location dictionaries under a `blacklist` concept and the two common-term dictionaries under a `whitelist` concept, then parameterized a single dictionary scan over both.
The unified framing was load-bearing only for the unified scan; with each dictionary now paired to its own intimately-named scan, the umbrella adds grouping without carrying weight.


=== PHI Categories

[[apcsgc_phi_category]]
{apcsgc_phi_category}::
The HIPAA Safe Harbor category attached to findings that identify PHI.
Determines the
{apcsnp_placeholder}
text used in
{apcsnt_anonymized_text}.
Enumerated: NAME, PROVIDER, FACILITY, DATE, DOB, PHONE, EMAIL, SSN, MRN, ACCOUNT, ENCOUNTER_ID, ADDRESS, HEALTH_PLAN_ID, DEVICE_ID, NPI, DEA, URL, IP_ADDRESS.
+
Container discerners carry their own native taxonomies (Stanford's 8-label flat set; spaCy's POS tags, dependency relations, and scispaCy biomedical entity labels; Stanza's Universal Dependencies POS tags, morphological features, dependency relations, and OntoNotes NER labels).
Mapping native taxonomies to the
{apcsgc_phi_category}
enumeration is a
{apcsnc_combining}
concern and is deferred along with the rest of combining.


== Normalization

[[apcsgn_normalization]]
{apcsgn_normalization}::
The one-time light-cleanup procedure that produces the canonical input for every discerner.
Consumes
{apcsuc_clipboard_content}
— parsed as
{apcsue_epic_html}
into a
{apcsdo_document}
when the content is HTML, or taken as-is when plain text — and produces
{apcsgt_normalized_text}.
One of the three named procedures of the pipeline, together with
{apcsnc_combining}
and
{apcsna_anonymization}.
Runs entirely inside the Rust application.
Internal mechanics (HTML stripping, whitespace collapse, line-break handling, encoding normalization) are implementation detail; the vocabulary concerns only the procedure's inputs and output.

[[apcsgt_normalized_text]]
{apcsgt_normalized_text}::
The canonical string every discerner consumes.
The output of
{apcsgn_normalization}.
Regions within
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
are the addressable units of detection; character positions are counted into this string, not into
{apcsuc_clipboard_content}
or
{apcsue_epic_html}.
All ten discerners are pure functions of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}.


== Placement and the Container

[[apcsgp_placement]]
{apcsgp_placement}::
The execution site at which a discerner runs.
Enumerated: `rust_application` (the discerner runs in-process inside the Tauri-hosted Rust application, emitting findings via direct function calls) and `container` (the discerner runs inside
{apcscc_container},
emitting findings via a JSON file written to the bind-mounted directory).
Every discerner declares its
{apcsgp_placement}
as part of its definition.
{apcsnc_combining}
reads both streams into the same
{apcsde_evidence}
pool;
from combining's perspective the
{apcsgp_placement}
of a finding's originating discerner is a provenance field, not a structural dimension.

[[apcscc_container]]
{apcscc_container}::
A long-running Linux container that hosts the three sophisticated discerners —
{apcscs_stanford},
{apcscs_spacy},
and
{apcscs_stanza}
— in their native Python runtime.
Parameterized by the set of discerner models it bundles (see APCPS for the concrete images, versions, and Dockerfile).
Exposes no network interface (`--network=none`), runs as a non-root user, and drops all Linux capabilities.
Lifecycle is managed by tabtargets, not by the Rust application — start, stop, and image build are human-directed actions, distinct from Clipbuddy startup.
Communication with the Rust application is governed by
{apcswp_wire};
see that section for transaction shapes, member types, and the call/return discipline.
See APCPS for the concrete bind-mount directory path, Dockerfile, and tabtarget set.


== Wire Protocol

The Rust application and
{apcscc_container}
are two
{axo_actor_s}
with independent lifecycles and no shared process memory.
They coordinate through a bilateral discipline — a *protocol* — whose structure is owned by this section.
The protocol names what can be exchanged and under what presumptions;
it does not name which filesystem directory or which image tag realizes it (those live in APCPS).

[[apcswp_wire]]
//axi_protocol axd_file_bus axd_disjoint_lifecycle
{apcswp_wire}::
The call/return discipline between the caller (Rust application) and the callee
({apcscc_container}).
+
*Transport* is
{axd_file_bus}:
a shared host directory is the wire;
the caller writes call envelopes as files and observes return envelopes as atomic file appearances;
the callee symmetrically observes calls and writes returns.
The directory path is a deployment concern named by APCPS, not by this protocol.
+
*Lifecycle presumption* is
{axd_disjoint_lifecycle}:
the caller cannot assume the callee exists at any point;
each transaction must tolerate callee-absent and callee-restart states;
the callee cannot assume caller persistence either — callers come and go.
+
*Concurrency* is single-tenant by construction:
at most one transaction is in flight at any instant.
The caller issues one call, awaits its return, then may issue the next.
No pipelining, no multiplexing.
+
*Direction* is synchronous from the caller's point of view:
a call is meaningful only when paired with its return;
the caller blocks (or times out) waiting for the callee's atomic reply appearance.
+
The protocol supports two named transactions — a readiness probe and the detection exchange — defined as structured children at the
{axl_detail_site}
(see subdocument).
Type terms referenced by transaction members are defined below.

=== Types

[[apcswt_transaction_index]]
//axt_number
{apcswt_transaction_index}::
A monotonically increasing integer assigned by the caller to each transaction within a session.
The index disambiguates concurrent-in-time but not-in-flight transactions at the filesystem level:
the caller writes call envelopes at index N;
the callee writes return envelopes at the same N;
the caller then advances to N+1.
+
Index is session-local — it resets to its starting value whenever the caller starts fresh.
The callee does not persist an expected index across its own restarts;
under
{axd_disjoint_lifecycle}
the callee accepts whatever index the caller presents and echoes it in the return.
Collision detection (a caller observing a return envelope it did not request) is the caller's signal that the callee was restarted mid-session.

[[apcswt_normalized_text]]
{apcswt_normalized_text}::
The wire-facing view of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}:
the exact string the caller wishes the callee to analyze.
Carried verbatim — the protocol imposes no additional normalization beyond what produced
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
upstream.
Size is bounded only by filesystem and runtime limits;
APCPS documents the practical upper bound for the container's current model configuration.

[[apcswt_finding_list]]
//axd_repeated
{apcswt_finding_list}::
The wire-facing view of the callee's consolidated output:
an ordered list of
{apcsgf_finding_s}
produced by the three container-side discerners
({apcscs_stanford},
{apcscs_spacy},
{apcscs_stanza})
running as pure functions of the call's
{apcswt_normalized_text}.
Each element carries its originating discerner as a provenance field so the caller's
{apcsnc_combining}
step can attribute it correctly.
Empty list is valid (all discerners produced no findings);
absence of the field is not distinguished from empty list.

[[apcswt_model_status]]
{apcswt_model_status}::
The callee's readiness self-report, returned in the readiness transaction's return envelope.
Carries per-discerner boolean readiness flags and — when any discerner is not yet ready — a short diagnostic string naming the unloaded model or the phase the callee is in (loading weights, warming JIT, etc.).
The protocol does not enumerate the status vocabulary;
the callee's publication and the caller's interpretation share a version compatibility contract documented in APCPS alongside the container image tag.

=== Protocol Detail

include::APCSWP-wire.adoc[leveloffset=+1]


== Discerners

Each discerner is an independent algorithm that consumes
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
and emits an intimately-named finding type.
Ten discerners run in parallel on the same input; no discerner refines or filters another's output.
{apcsnc_combining}
fuses their finding streams.

There is deliberately no generic supertype for discerners and no generic finding type.
Each discerner is named individually, parameterized individually, and reasoned about individually;
{apcsnc_combining}
rules reference discerners and findings by name, not by a shared abstract shape.
This is load-bearing: earlier drafts that introduced `algorithm` and `reliability_profile` abstractions collapsed distinct concerns (what a discerner knows, how confident it is, how its output should be displayed) into non-load-bearing abstractions that each had to be re-specialized at every rule site.

[[apcsgf_finding]]
{apcsgf_finding}::
A single detection emitted by any discerner, covering a region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}.
Prose noun — there is no generic schema.
Each discerner's
{apcsgf_finding}
subtype has its own intimate schema carrying whatever information that discerner's algorithm produces.

[[apcsde_evidence]]
{apcsde_evidence}::
The pool of every
{apcsgf_finding}
emitted by every discerner for a single input.
{apcsnc_combining}
is a function of
{apcsde_evidence}.
The pool preserves provenance — a finding's originating discerner is never lost — and allows multiple findings to cover the same region without any one being discarded or subordinated before combining.

=== Regex Scan

[[apcsds_regex]]
{apcsds_regex}::
The discerner that detects structurally identifiable PHI by regular-expression pattern matching.
Parameterized by the
{apcsdt_regex_table}.
Scans
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
against every pattern.
Each match emits one
{apcsdf_regex}
with the
{apcsgc_phi_category}
specified by the pattern entry.
{apcsgp_placement}
: `rust_application`.

[[apcsdf_regex]]
{apcsdf_regex}::
The output unit of
{apcsds_regex}.
Carries: the matched text, the region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
it covers, and the
{apcsgc_phi_category}
specified by the pattern that fired.

=== Label Scan

[[apcsds_label]]
{apcsds_label}::
The discerner that extracts PHI positionally from recognized Epic labels.
Parameterized by the
{apcsdt_label_registry}.
For each registry entry whose label text appears in
{apcsgt_normalized_text},
segment the value following the label into words and emit one
{apcsdf_label}
per word with the
{apcsgc_phi_category}
specified by the registry entry.
{apcsgp_placement}
: `rust_application`.

[[apcsdf_label]]
{apcsdf_label}::
The output unit of
{apcsds_label}.
Carries: the word text, the region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
it covers, the
{apcsgc_phi_category}
specified by the registry entry, and the originating label (retained for audit and for combining rules that reference specific labels).

=== Surname Scan

[[apcsds_surname]]
{apcsds_surname}::
The discerner that detects surname dictionary membership.
Parameterized by the
{apcsdd_surname}
(case-insensitive lookup via aho-corasick automaton).
Scans
{apcsgt_normalized_text};
every match emits one
{apcsdf_surname}.
The scan emits unconditionally on match — whitelist corroboration from
{apcsds_english}
or
{apcsds_medical}
is a
{apcsnc_combining}
concern, never a gate on emission here.
{apcsgp_placement}
: `rust_application`.

[[apcsdf_surname]]
{apcsdf_surname}::
The output unit of
{apcsds_surname}.
Carries: the matched text and the region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
it covers.
Implied
{apcsgc_phi_category}
: NAME.

=== Firstname Scan

[[apcsds_firstname]]
{apcsds_firstname}::
The discerner that detects first-name dictionary membership.
Parameterized by the
{apcsdd_firstname}
(case-insensitive lookup via aho-corasick automaton).
Scans
{apcsgt_normalized_text};
every match emits one
{apcsdf_firstname}.
Unconditional emission, like
{apcsds_surname}.
{apcsgp_placement}
: `rust_application`.

[[apcsdf_firstname]]
{apcsdf_firstname}::
The output unit of
{apcsds_firstname}.
Carries: the matched text and the region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
it covers.
Implied
{apcsgc_phi_category}
: NAME.

=== City Scan

[[apcsds_city]]
{apcsds_city}::
The discerner that detects city dictionary membership.
Parameterized by the
{apcsdd_city}
(case-insensitive lookup via aho-corasick automaton).
Scans
{apcsgt_normalized_text};
every match emits one
{apcsdf_city}.
Unconditional emission.
{apcsgp_placement}
: `rust_application`.

[[apcsdf_city]]
{apcsdf_city}::
The output unit of
{apcsds_city}.
Carries: the matched text and the region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
it covers.
Implied
{apcsgc_phi_category}
: ADDRESS (subject to remapping in combining — a city name overlapping a Stanford-labeled HOSPITAL span, for instance, may unify as FACILITY).

=== English Scan

[[apcsds_english]]
{apcsds_english}::
The discerner that detects common-English-word dictionary membership.
Parameterized by the
{apcsdw_english}
(case-insensitive lookup via aho-corasick automaton).
Scans
{apcsgt_normalized_text};
every match emits one
{apcsdf_english}.
Findings from this scan are suppression evidence — they indicate a word has a recognized non-PHI reading — and enter
{apcsde_evidence}
as peers to the name/location findings rather than gating them.
{apcsgp_placement}
: `rust_application`.

[[apcsdf_english]]
{apcsdf_english}::
The output unit of
{apcsds_english}.
Carries: the matched text and the region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
it covers.
No implied
{apcsgc_phi_category}
— this finding asserts common-English membership, not a PHI classification.

=== Medical Scan

[[apcsds_medical]]
{apcsds_medical}::
The discerner that detects medical-term dictionary membership.
Parameterized by the
{apcsdw_medical}
(case-insensitive lookup via aho-corasick automaton).
Scans
{apcsgt_normalized_text};
every match emits one
{apcsdf_medical}.
Like
{apcsds_english},
a source of suppression evidence — medical jargon is emphatically not PHI, even when it collides with a surname ("Lennox," "Broca," "Parkinson").
{apcsgp_placement}
: `rust_application`.

[[apcsdf_medical]]
{apcsdf_medical}::
The output unit of
{apcsds_medical}.
Carries: the matched text and the region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
it covers.
No implied
{apcsgc_phi_category}.

=== Stanford Scan

[[apcscs_stanford]]
{apcscs_stanford}::
The discerner that detects PHI by neural named-entity recognition using `StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base`.
Parameterized by the model's flat 8-label taxonomy:
`O, VENDOR, DATE, HCW, HOSPITAL, ID, PATIENT, PHONE`
(labels are flat — no BIO prefix — so runs of identical labels form a single entity).
Observed strengths (recorded from spike evaluation on the APCK synthetic fixtures): multi-token person names merged as a single span
(`Margaret J. Thornton` → one `PATIENT` span),
facility names merged
(`Maine Medical Center` → one `HOSPITAL` span),
narrative-scope catches where positional anchors are absent.
Observed weaknesses: structured identifiers (SSN, URL, address, device serial) fragment across multiple labels and are less reliable than
{apcsds_regex}
for the same categories.
The two are complementary, and
{apcsnc_combining}
is where the complementarity is cashed in.
Runs once per input inside the
{apcscc_container};
emits a
{apcscf_stanford}
per contiguous labeled span.
{apcsgp_placement}
: `container`.

[[apcscf_stanford]]
{apcscf_stanford}::
The output unit of
{apcscs_stanford}.
Carries: the matched text, the region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
it covers, the native Stanford entity label (one of the seven non-`O` labels above), and the model's confidence score for that span.
The native label is preserved verbatim; mapping to
{apcsgc_phi_category}
happens in
{apcsnc_combining}.

=== spaCy Scan

[[apcscs_spacy]]
{apcscs_spacy}::
The discerner that produces syntactic and biomedical-entity analysis via spaCy parameterized by a clinical-domain model (scispaCy's `en_core_sci_md`; see APCPS for the concrete pipeline component list).
Emits per-token structural signals (POS tag, morphological features, lemma, dependency head index, dependency relation) and any biomedical named-entity spans the pipeline produces.
The syntactic signals are the load-bearing output for this discerner's role in combining — the "may/May/may" disambiguation relies on POS tags separating modal verbs (AUX) from proper nouns (PROPN).
Runs once per input inside the
{apcscc_container};
emits a
{apcscf_spacy}
per token and per entity span.
{apcsgp_placement}
: `container`.

[[apcscf_spacy]]
{apcscf_spacy}::
The output unit of
{apcscs_spacy}.
Two variants share this name:
per-token findings carry text, region, POS tag, morphological features, lemma, and dependency information;
per-entity findings carry text, region, and the biomedical entity label.
Concrete JSON schema for both variants lives in APCPS.

=== Stanza Scan

[[apcscs_stanza]]
{apcscs_stanza}::
The discerner that produces syntactic and general-domain named-entity analysis via Stanza, parameterized by the English Universal Dependencies pipeline (see APCPS for concrete version).
Emits the full set of per-token signals Stanza's pipeline produces (Universal POS tag, language-specific POS tag, morphological features, lemma, dependency head, dependency relation, BIO-encoded NER tag) and entity-level spans derived from the BIO-tagged tokens.
Like
{apcscs_spacy},
chosen for its POS and dependency signals; its general-domain NER (OntoNotes-style PERSON/ORG/GPE labels) supplements
{apcscs_stanford}
with a second NER signal trained on different data.
Runs once per input inside the
{apcscc_container};
emits
{apcscf_stanza_s}
at both per-token and per-entity granularities.
{apcsgp_placement}
: `container`.

[[apcscf_stanza]]
{apcscf_stanza}::
The output unit of
{apcscs_stanza}.
As with
{apcscf_spacy},
per-token and per-entity variants share the name;
the concrete JSON schema for both lives in APCPS.


== Combining

NOTE: **Deferred section.**
The combining algorithm — the rules that fuse the ten discerner finding streams into authoritative per-region classifications — is deferred.
The decision to defer is deliberate: combining rules should be written with empirical
{apcsde_evidence}
in hand from real clinical text processed through all ten discerners, not invented before the signals are visible.
The quoins below are placeholders so downstream procedures have referenceable inputs and so the document's link graph remains complete; their authoritative shape will be written in a later pace once representative inputs have flowed through the pipeline.

Specifically deferred:

* The noun for what
{apcsnc_combining}
produces per region (candidates under consideration include verdict, decision, call, outcome, determination, or remaining as
{apcsnu_unified_finding}).
* Per-discerner-pair precedence rules and the complete combining rule table.
* Granularity handling when findings of different sizes overlap (phrase-scoped Stanford findings covering word-scoped label findings, etc.).
* The concrete mapping from
{apcscs_stanford}'s
native labels onto the
{apcsgc_phi_category}
enumeration, and similar mappings for spaCy and Stanza native taxonomies.
* Clinician-toggle composition — whether clinician decisions enter combining as an additional finding stream, operate as a post-combining mutator on
{apcsnu_unified_finding_s},
or take some third shape.
* Second-order reprocessing — whether phrase-scoped findings derive additional word-scoped findings for uniform word-grained combining, while preserving the original phrase-scoped finding as evidence.

[[apcsnc_combining]]
{apcsnc_combining}::
The procedure that consumes
{apcsde_evidence}
and produces
{apcsnu_unified_finding_s}
covering regions of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}.
One of the three named procedures of the pipeline, together with
{apcsgn_normalization}
and
{apcsna_anonymization}.
Rules are deferred (see above).
The procedure is named
{apcsnc_combining}
rather than the earlier draft name `reconciliation` because the latter implied smoothing competing verdicts into one, whereas the intent is to read all evidence in parallel and produce a classification per region without discarding any finding's contribution to the record.

[[apcsnu_unified_finding]]
{apcsnu_unified_finding}::
The output of
{apcsnc_combining}
for a single region of
{apcsgt_normalized_text}:
a single authoritative classification consumed by
{apcsna_anonymization}.
The concrete shape of a
{apcsnu_unified_finding}
(the fields carried, the classification vocabulary, the clinician-toggle composition) is deferred.


== Anonymization

[[apcsna_anonymization]]
{apcsna_anonymization}::
The procedure that consumes
{apcsnu_unified_finding_s}
and
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
and produces
{apcsnt_anonymized_text}.
Regions classified as PHI are replaced by their category-specific
{apcsnp_placeholder_s};
regions classified as retained text pass through verbatim.
Runs entirely inside the Rust application.
One of the three named procedures of the pipeline, named for symmetry with
{apcsgn_normalization}
and
{apcsnc_combining}.

[[apcsnt_anonymized_text]]
{apcsnt_anonymized_text}::
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
with every region marked for elision in the set of
{apcsnu_unified_finding_s}
replaced by its
{apcsnp_placeholder}.
The output format for pasting into Open Evidence.

[[apcsnp_placeholder]]
{apcsnp_placeholder}::
A category-specific substitution string emitted by
{apcsna_anonymization}.
Format: `[CATEGORY]` (e.g., `[NAME]`, `[PHONE]`, `[ADDRESS]`).
Exception: DOB emits `Age: NN` (computed from date of birth and current date; ages over 89 emit `Age: 90+` per HIPAA Safe Harbor).


== Diagnostic Projections

The assay projections render the pipeline's internal state for review and debugging.
They are not user-facing — the user-facing surface is
{apcsnt_anonymized_text}
together with the triage view (owned by APCAS).
Two sibling projections exist because the pipeline now has two meaningful stages to inspect: the raw per-discerner
{apcsde_evidence}
before
{apcsnc_combining},
and the fused view after.

[[apcsud_discerner_assay]]
{apcsud_discerner_assay}::
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
annotated inline with markers drawn from
{apcsde_evidence}:
every
{apcsgf_finding}
from every discerner is rendered, with its originating discerner and any native tag it carries distinguishable in the marker.
Shows each discerner's raw output — overlaps, disagreements, and granularity differences are all visible side-by-side, which is the point.
The exact marker content per discerner varies with what each discerner's
{apcsgf_finding}
carries (see
{apcsum_elision_marker});
the format is a debugging aid, not a stable interface.

[[apcsuu_unified_assay]]
{apcsuu_unified_assay}::
{apcsgt_normalized_text}
with every region of every
{apcsnu_unified_finding}
annotated inline using
{apcsum_elision_marker_s}.
Shows the post-combining view — what
{apcsna_anonymization}
actually sees.

[[apcsum_elision_marker]]
{apcsum_elision_marker}::
The annotation unit in an
{apcsud_discerner_assay}
or an
{apcsuu_unified_assay}.
In an
{apcsud_discerner_assay}
the format is `‹DISCERNER:TAG text›` (e.g., `‹REGEX:SSN 123-45-6789›`, `‹SURNAME:NAME Thornton›`, `‹STANFORD:PATIENT Margaret Thornton›`, `‹SPACY:AUX may›`) so each discerner's contribution is distinguishable;
the TAG slot carries the discerner's native tag (PHI category, native entity label, POS tag, etc.) or is omitted for discerners whose findings have no meaningful tag (e.g., the
{apcsds_english}
and
{apcsds_medical},
where membership itself is the finding and rendered as `‹ENGLISH may›` or `‹MEDICAL aspirin›`).
In an
{apcsuu_unified_assay}
the format is `‹CATEGORY text›` — the discerner that originated the region is absorbed into the combining decision and no longer shown.
Guillemets delimit the marker to avoid collision with clinical prose punctuation.

3-## Scope
4-
5:[Omitted long matching line]
6-
7-This is a living document. The pipeline will evolve rapidly during prototype development. Changes here do not require parallel updates to the product spec.
--
32-| File watching | `notify` | latest | MIT/Apache | FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChanges on Windows |
33-| NSPasteboard FFI (macOS) | `objc2` + `objc2-foundation` + `objc2-app-kit` | 0.6.4 / 0.3.2 / 0.3.2 | MIT | Declared-UTI enumeration for multi-flavor clipboard harvest. Versions pinned to match whatever Tauri already pulls transitively (single objc2 tree resolution). Gated `[target.'cfg(target_os = "macos")'.dependencies]`. |
34:| JSON deserialization | `serde` + `serde_json` | 1.x | MIT/Apache | Read container's consolidated findings file |
35-
36-All Rust crates are pure Rust, fully Cargo-lockable on macOS and Windows. Tauri requires the system webview (WebKit on macOS, WebView2 on Windows — both ship with the OS). The webview receives pre-rendered HTML from Rust on every state change; no JavaScript application code exists in the project.
--
251-### Container Discerners — parameterization
252-
253:All three run inside `apcscc_container` (see Container Architecture below). Each consumes `{N}-in.txt` (the normalized text) and contributes a key to the consolidated `{N}.json`.
254-
255-| Discerner | Model | Placement | Primary output signal |
--
280-**Stanza choice.** English Universal Dependencies pipeline. Provides a second, architecturally independent syntactic analyzer plus OntoNotes-style NER (PERSON, ORG, GPE, DATE). The two-parser redundancy is deliberate — when spaCy and Stanza agree that "may" is AUX, that's independent corroboration; when they disagree, combining can weight accordingly. Whether both ship in MVP-to-Ann or whether evaluation narrows to one is a decision for a later pace.
281-
282:## Container Architecture
283-
284-### Purpose
--
313-**Indexing discipline.** The container scans `$HOME/apcjd/` for `{N}-in.txt` files. It processes **only the highest-indexed** input and ignores all others. This implements a drop-old backlog policy: if Ann copies three things quickly, the container processes only the newest; older inputs are skipped. Cleanup of stale inputs and outputs is manual.
314-
315:### Wire Protocol Realization
316-
317-The wire's abstract contract — transactions, envelopes, types, and the single-tenant synchronous discipline — is defined by `apcswp_wire` in APCS0. This subsection documents the prototype's concrete realization of that contract on top of the `$HOME/apcjd/` bind mount.
--
323-| Transaction | Caller writes | Callee writes |
324-|-------------|---------------|---------------|
325:| Detection | `{N}-in.txt` (plain UTF-8 — `apcswt_normalized_text`) | `{N}.json` (per schema below — carries `apcswt_transaction_index` and `apcswt_finding_list`) |
326:| Readiness probe | `ping-in` (reserved name, non-indexed) | `ping.json` (carries `apcswt_model_status`) |
327-
328:The readiness probe's filenames are placeholders; the concrete names are a realization decision pending implementation. The detection transaction uses `{N}-in.txt` / `{N}.json` because the index serves double duty as filename disambiguator and as `apcswt_transaction_index`.
329-
330-**Atomic publish.** The container writes `{N}.json.tmp` during assembly, then `rename(2)`s to `{N}.json` when all three discerners have completed. POSIX guarantees rename atomicity within the same filesystem — Clipbuddy either sees the file absent or sees it complete, never partially written.
--
334-[Omitted long context line]
335-
336:### Container Output JSON Schema
337-
338:This is the concrete JSON realization of the detection transaction's return envelope. APCS0 owns the abstract envelope: it carries `apcswt_transaction_index` (echoed) and `apcswt_finding_list` (the three-discerner output). This subsection defines how those types are serialized for the prototype.
339-
340-The `{N}.json` file has this top-level shape:
--
344-  "index": 10000,
345-  "stanford": {
346:    "findings": [ /* see Stanford finding schema */ ]
347-  },
348-  "spacy": {
349:    "findings": [ /* see spaCy finding schema */ ]
350-  },
351-  "stanza": {
352:    "findings": [ /* see Stanza finding schema */ ]
353-  }
354-}
355-```
356-
357:- `index` realizes `apcswt_transaction_index` — echoed from the call's filename `{N}-in.txt`.
358:- The `stanford`/`spacy`/`stanza` object is the prototype's shape for `apcswt_finding_list`: a per-discerner grouping rather than a flat list. Each grouping's `findings` array carries that discerner's findings tagged implicitly by the containing key (the schemas below do not repeat the discerner name on each finding — the parent key is the tag).
359-
360:No schema version field for the prototype — the shape is pinned to the container image version. If the schema evolves, the image tag changes, and any Clipbuddy built against an older schema is simply incompatible with the newer image.
361-
362:Per-discerner finding schemas:
363-
364-**Stanford finding:**
--
431-```
432-
433:These schemas are illustrative for the prototype — concrete field ordering, optionality, and type precision will be fixed by the Rust deserializer types in `apcd/src/` when the consumer is written.
434-
435-### Security Posture

content.content:
360	No schema version field for the prototype — the shape is pinned to the container image version. If the schema evolves, the image tag changes, and any Clipbuddy built against an older schema is simply incompatible with the newer image.
361	
362	Per-discerner finding schemas:
363	
364	**Stanford finding:**
365	```json
366	{
367	  "text": "Margaret J. Thornton",
368	  "start": 45,
369	  "end": 65,
370	  "label": "PATIENT",
371	  "confidence": 0.987
372	}
373	```
374	- `start`/`end` are character offsets into `apcsgt_normalized_text`.
375	- `label` is one of the 8 native labels (non-`O` values only; `O` tokens are not emitted as findings).
376	- `confidence` is the model's softmax confidence for the dominant label over the span.
377	
378	**spaCy finding.** Two variants share a single discerner namespace; each finding is either token-level or entity-level, distinguished by a `kind` field:
379	
380	```json
381	{
382	  "kind": "token",
383	  "text": "may",
384	  "start": 120,
385	  "end": 123,
386	  "pos": "AUX",
387	  "tag": "MD",
388	  "morph": "VerbType=Mod",
389	  "lemma": "may",
390	  "head": 5,
391	  "dep": "aux"
392	}
393	```
394	
395	```json
396	{
397	  "kind": "entity",
398	  "text": "aspirin",
399	  "start": 84,
400	  "end": 91,
401	  "label": "CHEMICAL"
402	}
403	```
404	
405	**Stanza finding.** Same two-variant pattern:
406	
407	```json
408	{
409	  "kind": "token",
410	  "text": "Margaret",
411	  "start": 45,
412	  "end": 53,
413	  "upos": "PROPN",
414	  "xpos": "NNP",
415	  "feats": "Number=Sing",
416	  "lemma": "Margaret",
417	  "head": 2,
418	  "deprel": "nsubj",
419	  "ner": "B-PERSON"
420	}
421	```
422	
423	```json
424	{
425	  "kind": "entity",
426	  "text": "Margaret J. Thornton",
427	  "start": 45,
428	  "end": 65,
429	  "label": "PERSON"
430	}
431	```
432	
433	These schemas are illustrative for the prototype — concrete field ordering, optionality, and type precision will be fixed by the Rust deserializer types in `apcd/src/` when the consumer is written.
434	
435	### Security Posture
436	
437	| Control | Setting | Rationale |
438	|---------|---------|-----------|

file.content:
No schema version field for the prototype — the shape is pinned to the container image version. If the schema evolves, the image tag changes, and any Clipbuddy built against an older schema is simply incompatible with the newer image.

Per-discerner finding schemas:

**Stanford finding:**
```json
{
  "text": "Margaret J. Thornton",
  "start": 45,
  "end": 65,
  "label": "PATIENT",
  "confidence": 0.987
}
```
- `start`/`end` are character offsets into `apcsgt_normalized_text`.
- `label` is one of the 8 native labels (non-`O` values only; `O` tokens are not emitted as findings).
- `confidence` is the model's softmax confidence for the dominant label over the span.

**spaCy finding.** Two variants share a single discerner namespace; each finding is either token-level or entity-level, distinguished by a `kind` field:

```json
{
  "kind": "token",
  "text": "may",
  "start": 120,
  "end": 123,
  "pos": "AUX",
  "tag": "MD",
  "morph": "VerbType=Mod",
  "lemma": "may",
  "head": 5,
  "dep": "aux"
}
```

```json
{
  "kind": "entity",
  "text": "aspirin",
  "start": 84,
  "end": 91,
  "label": "CHEMICAL"
}
```

**Stanza finding.** Same two-variant pattern:

```json
{
  "kind": "token",
  "text": "Margaret",
  "start": 45,
  "end": 53,
  "upos": "PROPN",
  "xpos": "NNP",
  "feats": "Number=Sing",
  "lemma": "Margaret",
  "head": 2,
  "deprel": "nsubj",
  "ner": "B-PERSON"
}
```

```json
{
  "kind": "entity",
  "text": "Margaret J. Thornton",
  "start": 45,
  "end": 65,
  "label": "PERSON"
}
```

These schemas are illustrative for the prototype — concrete field ordering, optionality, and type precision will be fixed by the Rust deserializer types in `apcd/src/` when the consumer is written.

### Security Posture

| Control | Setting | Rationale |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| Network | `--network=none` | The container cannot initiate or accept any network traffic — eliminates PHI exfiltration risk |

content.text:
Confirmed the gap. Here's what I see:

**AXLA already has rich record vocabulary.** The kit for this exists:
- `axr_record`, `axr_record_json`, `axr_member` — format-agnostic record/member patterns
- `axj_structure`, `axj_field`, `axj_field_tag`, `axj_field_type`, `axj_field_cardinality`, `axj_nested_field`, `axj_array_element`, `axj_root_structure`, `axj_type_reference` — JSON-specific
- `axt_*` primitives (string, number, boolean, array, object, map, enumeration, enum_value, variant, insignia)
- `axd_*` dimensions (required, optional, nullable, repeated, list, defaulted)
- Hierarchy markers `axhefm_motif_field` / `axheft_typed_field` for members within a record definition site

So we don't need to **build out** AXLA — it already supports this. The gap is on APCS0's side.

**APCS0 doesn't voice records.** The four `apcswt_*` type quoins (transaction_index, normalized_text, finding_list, model_status) are prose-described:

- `apcswt_finding_list` — "ordered list… each element carries its originating discerner as a provenance field" — no structure voiced
- `apcswt_model_status` — "per-discerner boolean readiness flags and… a short diagnostic string" — no structure voiced
- `apcscf_stanford`/`apcscf_spacy`/`apcscf_stanza` — "Carries: text, region, label, confidence" / "Concrete JSON schema lives in APCPS"

**APCPS has the actual JSON** — `{index, stanford:{findings:[...]}, spacy:{findings:[...]}, stanza:{findings:[...]}}` with per-discerner finding shapes — but with an explicit caveat: *"These schemas are illustrative for the prototype — concrete field ordering, optionality, and type precision will be fixed by the Rust deserializer types."* No AXLA voicings.

**Circular punt**: APCS0 says "schema lives in APCPS"; APCPS (after last pace) cites APCS0 for wire contract. The envelope shape is owned by APCS0 but the payload structure is owned by neither — it's an illustrative JSON blob in APCPS.

**Your instinct is right.** APCS0 top-level quoins for payload records is the natural move. Two candidate shapes:

1. **Enrich voicings of existing quoins.** Add `axr_record_json` voicing to `apcswt_finding_list`, `apcswt_model_status`, `apcscf_stanford`, `apcscf_spacy`, `apcscf_stanza` and declare members at their definition sites using `axhefm_`/`axheft_` hierarchy markers. No new quoins. Respects the existing `apcsw_` mapping-section note that "only p and t yield primary quoins in the apcsw_ domain" — records stay as voicings on type quoins, members stay as hierarchy markers.

2. **Mint record-level quoins.** Promote the existing note's "r is prose-only scaffolding" — mint `apcswr_*` primary quoins for each envelope/payload record. Heavier; only load-bearing if we want to cross-reference records from other documents.

**Question before we proceed**: two things to decide.

1. **Is `apcscf_spacy`/`apcscf_stanza` the right home for per-variant records, given each has two variants (token + entity) sharing one quoin?** The single-quoin-with-variants framing is load-bearing for the discerner concept but fights the record voicing (one record = one shape). Possible resolutions: voice the quoin as `axt_variant` containing two records, OR split into separate quoins per variant.

2. **Does this slot ahead of ₢A9AAe (wire-protocol-impl)?** It probably should — if we're formalizing the record shape, the Rust serde types in ₢A9AAe should comply with AXLA-voiced records, not with APCPS's illustrative JSON. That makes this a new pace between ₢A9AAd (just wrapped) and ₢A9AAe.

What's your reaction?

lastPrompt: We need an interval of chatting I think before we proceed.  In the previous pace, we developed a vocabulary for 'protocol' which is good as far as it goes but I now muse is incomplete, because we addi…

message.content: Yes APCPS is on the edge of being retired now that APCS0 is starting to take on maturity.  Now, to the protocol question: you will find that AXLA often has multiple ways of documenting something: one way is expressly for the 'top level' quoin first class definition, and the other way is more subtle where a 'top level subdocument' contains the full specification.  Mainly it is a question of the reach of the domain in question.  For example, the 'reach' of the protocol here is relatively limited, however the import of the carried json is indeed much more global, in that it must be sourced by the container but then processed intimately by the application.  Do we yet have record type hierarchy treatment for this?

content.text: Let me check what AXLA already has for record/JSON hierarchy treatment.

680	{axo_actor}::
681	A
682	{axl_motif}
683	representing a specific named identity with defined responsibilities and exclusive authority over an operational domain.
684	{axo_actor_s}
685	instantiate
686	{axo_identity}
687	with concrete bindings (human via OAuth, service via service account) and permissions.
688	
689	[[axo_entity]]
690	{axo_entity}::
691	A
692	{axl_motif}
693	representing an object with both structure and behavior.
694	Unlike
695	{axr_record}
696	which captures pure data,
697	{axo_entity}
698	includes methods, builder patterns, or lifecycle operations.
699	{axl_voicing_s}
700	describe the entity's data members and its behavioral interface (construction, mutation, registration).
701	Typical manifestations include Rust structs with `impl` blocks, builder-pattern APIs, or objects that participate in registration protocols.
702	
703	== Axial Type Terms
704	
705	[[axt_type]]
706	{axt_type}::
707	A
708	{axl_motif}
709	representing a data format constraint that defines valid values and structures.
710	Enforces data validation and enables type-safe operations.
711	
712	[[axt_string]]
713	{axt_string}::
714	A
715	{axl_motif}
716	representing text data type with optional pattern validation.
717	{axl_voicing_s}
718	may address context-specific concerns including:
719	+
720	* Encoding (ASCII, UTF-8, locale-dependent character sets)
721	* Termination semantics (null-terminated, length-prefixed, delimiter-bounded)
722	* Mutability (immutable values, mutable buffers)
723	* Memory management (owned, borrowed, reference-counted)
724	* Safety constraints (shell-safe, URL-safe, JSON-safe, SQL-safe)
725	* Format patterns (timestamps, identifiers, hashes, paths)
726	* Size and bounds (fixed-length, maximum length, unbounded)
727	
728	[[axt_number]]
729	{axt_number}::
730	A
731	{axl_motif}
732	representing numeric value type supporting integers and floating point.
733	May specify range constraints or precision requirements.
734	
735	[[axt_boolean]]
736	{axt_boolean}::
737	A
738	{axl_motif}
739	representing binary true/false value type.
740	Maps to language-specific boolean implementations.
741	
742	[[axt_array]]
743	{axt_array}::
744	A
745	{axl_motif}
746	representing ordered collection of typed elements.
747	Specifies element type and optional size constraints.
748	
749	[[axt_object]]
750	{axt_object}::
751	A
752	{axl_motif}
753	representing named field collection forming a composite type.
754	Contains typed fields with defined cardinality.
755	
756	[[axt_map]]
757	{axt_map}::
758	A
759	{axl_motif}
760	representing a dictionary structure with typed keys and typed values.
761	2-arity: reads two
762	{mcm_attribute_reference_s}
763	from following text — the first identifies the key type, the second identifies the value type.
764	Keys are unique within the map; values are accessed by key lookup.
765	Serialization format determines key ordering guarantees and key type constraints (e.g., JSON restricts keys to strings).
766	
767	[[axt_enumeration]]
768	{axt_enumeration}::
769	A
770	{axl_motif}
771	representing constrained set of valid string values.
772	Restricts values to predefined options.
773	
774	[[axt_enum_value]]
775	{axt_enum_value}::
776	A
777	{axl_motif}
778	representing single allowed option within an
779	{axt_enumeration}.
780	Must be explicitly listed in enumeration definition.
781	
782	[[axt_insignia]]
783	{axt_insignia}::
784	A
785	{axl_motif}
786	representing a glyph-prefixed typed identity string with seed-based allocation.
787	Extends
788	{axt_string}
789	with structural properties: a unicode verification glyph (decorative in input, mandatory in output), a fixed encoding format, a monotonic seed allocation mechanism, and optional hierarchical embedding (parent identity encoded as a prefix substring).
790	+
791	Every
792	{axt_insignia}
793	declares an encoding nature via
794	{axd_dimension_s}:
795	{axd_algebraic}
796	or
797	{axd_temporal}.
798	
799	[[axt_variant]]
800	{axt_variant}::
801	A
802	{axl_motif}
803	representing a value that can take one of several distinct forms, where each form may have different structure.
804	Unlike
805	{axt_enumeration}
806	which constrains to predefined strings,
807	{axt_variant}
808	allows structurally different alternatives.
809	{axl_voicing_s}
810	define the specific forms and discrimination logic for their context.
811	
812	== Axial Record Terms
813	
814	{axr_record_s}
815	and
816	{axr_member_s}
817	provide format-agnostic patterns for structured data.
818	Format-specific variants (JSON, protobuf, XML, etc.) capture serialization-specific semantics.
819	{axr_member_s}
820	inherit format context from their parent
821	{axr_record}.
822	
823	[[axr_record]]
824	{axr_record}::
825	A
826	{axl_motif}
827	representing a named collection of typed
828	{axr_member_s}.
829	Abstract pattern that format-specific variants voice with serialization semantics.
830	Use format-specific variants (
831	{axr_record_json},
832	{axr_record_protobuf},
833	etc.) when format is known.
834	
835	[[axr_record_json]]
836	{axr_record_json}::
837	A
838	{axl_motif}
839	representing a JSON object structure.
840	{axr_member_s}
841	are unordered, string-keyed properties.
842	Requires
843	{axd_nullable}
844	specification since JSON has explicit null.
845	
846	[[axr_record_protobuf]]
847	{axr_record_protobuf}::
848	A
849	{axl_motif}
850	representing a protobuf message.
851	{axr_member_s}
852	require
853	{axd_field_number}
854	and
855	{axd_wire_type}
856	dimensions.
857	Supports schema evolution through field numbering.
858	
859	[[axr_record_xml]]
860	{axr_record_xml}::
861	A
862	{axl_motif}
863	representing an XML element structure.
864	{axr_member_s}
865	require
866	{axd_xml_form}
867	to distinguish attributes from child elements.
868	Order may be significant depending on schema.
869	
870	[[axr_record_yaml]]
871	{axr_record_yaml}::
872	A
873	{axl_motif}
874	representing a YAML mapping.
875	Superset of JSON with anchors, aliases, and multi-document support.
876	{axr_member_s}
877	follow JSON-like semantics with extended syntax.
878	
879	[[axr_record_binary]]
880	{axr_record_binary}::
881	A
882	{axl_motif}
883	representing a binary/memory structure.
884	{axr_member_s}
885	require
886	{axd_endianness}
887	for multi-byte fields.
888	Fixed layout with explicit size and alignment.
889	
890	[[axr_member]]
891	{axr_member}::
892	A
893	{axl_motif}
894	representing a named, typed element within an
895	{axr_record}.
896	Inherits serialization format from parent
897	{axr_record}.
898	1-arity: reads one
899	{mcm_sprue}
900	from following text — the serialized property name (e.g., the JSON key).
901	The
902	{mcm_sprue}
903	is the exact wire-level name; the
904	{mcm_quoin}
905	is the conceptual name.
906	Additional type motifs on the annotation line (e.g.,
907	{axt_map},
908	{axt_string})
909	contribute their own arity to the total read sequence:
910	{axr_member}
911	sprue first, then type motif lookaheads.
912	Qualified by
913	{axd_dimension_s}
914	in
915	{axl_annotation}:
916	`//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axt_string`
917	
918	== Axial JSON Terms
919	
920	[[axj_structure]]
921	{axj_structure}::
922	A
923	{axl_motif}
924	representing named schema definition for JSON objects.
925	Defines complete object shape with field specifications.
926	
927	[[axj_field]]
928	{axj_field}::
929	A
930	{axl_motif}
931	representing named, typed element within a
932	{axj_structure}.
933	Combines tag identifier with type constraint.
934	
935	[[axj_field_tag]]
936	{axj_field_tag}::
937	A
938	{axl_motif}
939	representing the key identifier for a

28:// axhe*_: Axial Hierarchy Entity (standalone entity hierarchy markers) → mcm_form_deflist
29-/ xref_: External specification references
30-//

--
180-// Axial Voicing Entity Terms (definition-site entity annotations)
181-:axve_entity:                         <<axve_entity,Entity Voicing>>

183:// Axial Hierarchy Entity Terms (standalone entity hierarchy markers)
184::axheb_entity:                        <<axheb_entity,Hierarchy Entity>>
185::axheft_typed_field:                  <<axheft_typed_field,Hierarchy Typed Field>>
186::axhefm_motif_field:                  <<axhefm_motif_field,Hierarchy Motif Field>>
187::axhems_scoped_method:                <<axhems_scoped_method,Hierarchy Scoped Method>>

content.text:
**Answer: No, AXLA does not yet have record-hierarchy subdocument treatment.**

AXLA currently has four standalone-hierarchy-marker families, each earning its subdocument pattern because structure + relationships exceed what's expressible inline:

| Family | Opens | Purpose |
|--------|-------|---------|
| `axhr*_` | `axhrb_regime` | Regime subdocs — variables, groups, gates, kit operations |
| `axho*_` | `axhob_operation` | Operation subdocs — parameters, outputs, steps |
| `axhp*_` | `axhpb_protocol` | Protocol subdocs — transactions, calls, returns (minted ₢A9AAd) |
| `axhe*_` | `axheb_entity` | Entity subdocs — fields (Layer 1) + methods (Layer 2) |

The closest adjacent is `axhe*_`. It has `axheft_typed_field` / `axhefm_motif_field` which are the right *shape* of markers for record members. But it's anchored on `axheb_entity`, which AXLA defines explicitly against records:

> `{axo_entity}` — an object with both structure and behavior. Unlike `{axr_record}` which captures pure data, `{axo_entity}` includes methods, builder patterns, or lifecycle operations.

Using entity markers for pure-data records would be conceptual pollution — the same mismatch that motivated minting `axhp*_` last pace rather than pressing `axho*_` into service for protocols.

**What's needed**: a new `axh{?}*_` family dedicated to records. Letter constraint: `r` is taken (regime), `e` is taken (entity). Free candidates with mnemonic:

- `axhc*_` — reCord (c works when r is unavailable; parallels `apcsc_` where c stands for container in APCS0)
- `axhd*_` — Data record

Estimated family shape, mirroring `axhe*_`'s discipline:

| Marker | Lookahead | Purpose |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| `axhcb_record` | 1 attr ref | Opens record block at detail site |
| `axhcft_typed_field` | 1 attr ref | Field whose type reuses existing type/record quoin (supports nesting by pointing at another record's anchor) |
| `axhcfm_motif_field` | 0 | Field whose type is motif-only on annotation line |

Variants and arrays handled by dimensions on the annotation line (`axd_repeated`, `axd_nullable`, `axt_variant` with motif lookaheads) — same discipline the entity family uses.

**Then the APCS0 cascade**:

1. Revise the `apcsw_` legend note ("r is prose-only scaffolding, not a primary quoin"). Promote `r` to primary so we can mint `apcswr_*` quoins.
2. Mint record quoins for each wire payload shape — candidates: `apcswr_detection_return`, `apcswr_probe_return`, `apcswr_finding_stanford`, `apcswr_finding_spacy` (voiced `axt_variant` to cover token + entity), `apcswr_finding_stanza` (same variant treatment), `apcswr_model_status`.
3. Each record's detail site is a subdocument (APCSWR-payloads.adoc or one per record, depending on size) using the new `axhc*_` markers.
4. APCPS's "Container Output JSON Schema" section becomes obsolete — retired as part of the APCPS wind-down you mentioned.

**Two structural questions before we proceed**:

1. **Where does the hierarchy family live?** The protocol family was minted in AXLA because it's generally reusable. Records are arguably *more* universally reusable — every kit with JSON, configs, or wire formats wants this. Argument for AXLA-first. Counter-argument: minting AXLA infrastructure on a narrow proving case risks over-generalization. Which reach-bet do you prefer?

2. **Where do the record quoins live in APCS0?** Three options:
   - **Under `apcsw_`** — promote `r` to primary (wire payload records)
   - **Under `apcsc_`** — `apcscr_*` (the container produces them)
   - **Under a new domain letter** — `apcsj_*` for JSON records (dedicated)
   
   The `apcsw_` option keeps wire concerns colocated; `apcsc_` emphasizes authorship; a new letter admits that records have their own reach distinct from wire vs. container.

What's your read?

lastPrompt: Yes APCPS is on the edge of being retired now that APCS0 is starting to take on maturity.  Now, to the protocol question: you will find that AXLA often has multiple ways of documenting something: one…

message.content: I may be off topic: too many ideas in my head.  Rather than a subdocument format for record (including json style) I want to know how AXLA would describe/ voice a 'top level' json structure?

content.content:
1	==== Decision
2	
3	Cloud Build configuration files for trigger-based builds use `cloudbuild.json` format,
4	generated programmatically via `jq`.
5	YAML is not used.
6	
7	==== Rationale
8	
9	JSON is the Cloud Build REST API's native wire format.
10	The `projects.builds.create` and `projects.triggers.run` endpoints accept JSON request bodies
11	modeled on the API's `Build` resource.
12	YAML is a file-authoring convenience that gets parsed into the same `Build` structure before submission.
13	
14	Programmatic generation via `jq` produces correct JSON directly — no YAML indentation
15	fragility, no multiline string quoting hazards, no dependency on `yq` or custom emitters.
16	The stitch function (`zrbf_stitch_build_json`) already generates JSON;
17	converting to YAML would add complexity with zero functional benefit.
18	
19	==== Evidence
20	
21	[cols="1,3",options="header"]
22	|===
23	|Claim
24	|Source
25	
26	|REST API is natively JSON
27	|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.builds/create[projects.builds.create API reference] — request body is a `Build` resource in JSON
28	
29	|YAML and JSON are equivalent serializations
30	|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/build-config-file-schema[Build configuration file schema] — "You can write the build config file using the YAML or the JSON syntax"
31	
32	|Triggers accept both formats
33	|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/create-manage-triggers[Create and manage build triggers] — "Cloud Build configuration file (yaml or json)"
34	
35	|Trigger autodetect precedence includes JSON
36	|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.triggers[projects.triggers REST resource] — precedence: `cloudbuild.yaml`, `cloudbuild.yml`, `cloudbuild.json`, `Dockerfile`
37	
38	|No deprecation signals through Feb 2026
39	|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/release-notes[Cloud Build release notes] — no mention of reducing JSON support
40	
41	|SLSA provenance does not differ by format
42	|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance[Generate and validate build provenance] — provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` (file name), not format-specific fields
43	|===
44	
45	==== What Dies
46	
47	* Any assumption that `cloudbuild.yaml` is required for triggers or SLSA provenance
48	* The linked term `rbtgr_build_yaml` — replaced by
49	{rbtgr_build_json}
50	* YAML-specific concerns: multiline string quoting, indentation sensitivity, `$$` escaping conventions
51	
52	==== What Survives
53	
54	* `zrbf_stitch_build_json()` — continues to emit JSON via `jq`, adapted for trigger structure
55	* `rbgjb/*.sh` step scripts — inlined into JSON `args` arrays at inscribe time
56	* All `_RBGY_*` substitution variables — baked into JSON `substitutions` object
57	* OCI Layout Bridge pattern — unchanged
58	
59	==== Provenance Note
60	
61	SLSA v1.0 provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` which will contain `cloudbuild.json`.
62	This is a cosmetic difference from `cloudbuild.yaml` — the structured provenance fields
63	(build inputs, parameters, outputs) are identical regardless of config file format.
64	
65	==== References
66	
67	* Research conducted: 2026-02-24 (web search + API documentation review)
68	* Google docs consistently present YAML examples by convention, not by requirement
69	* The `gcloud builds submit` CLI internally converts any config format to JSON for API submission
70

file.content:
==== Decision

Cloud Build configuration files for trigger-based builds use `cloudbuild.json` format,
generated programmatically via `jq`.
YAML is not used.

==== Rationale

JSON is the Cloud Build REST API's native wire format.
The `projects.builds.create` and `projects.triggers.run` endpoints accept JSON request bodies
modeled on the API's `Build` resource.
YAML is a file-authoring convenience that gets parsed into the same `Build` structure before submission.

Programmatic generation via `jq` produces correct JSON directly — no YAML indentation
fragility, no multiline string quoting hazards, no dependency on `yq` or custom emitters.
The stitch function (`zrbf_stitch_build_json`) already generates JSON;
converting to YAML would add complexity with zero functional benefit.

==== Evidence

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Claim
|Source

|REST API is natively JSON
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.builds/create[projects.builds.create API reference] — request body is a `Build` resource in JSON

|YAML and JSON are equivalent serializations
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/build-config-file-schema[Build configuration file schema] — "You can write the build config file using the YAML or the JSON syntax"

|Triggers accept both formats
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/create-manage-triggers[Create and manage build triggers] — "Cloud Build configuration file (yaml or json)"

|Trigger autodetect precedence includes JSON
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.triggers[projects.triggers REST resource] — precedence: `cloudbuild.yaml`, `cloudbuild.yml`, `cloudbuild.json`, `Dockerfile`

|No deprecation signals through Feb 2026
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/release-notes[Cloud Build release notes] — no mention of reducing JSON support

|SLSA provenance does not differ by format
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance[Generate and validate build provenance] — provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` (file name), not format-specific fields
|===

==== What Dies

* Any assumption that `cloudbuild.yaml` is required for triggers or SLSA provenance
* The linked term `rbtgr_build_yaml` — replaced by
{rbtgr_build_json}
* YAML-specific concerns: multiline string quoting, indentation sensitivity, `$$` escaping conventions

==== What Survives

* `zrbf_stitch_build_json()` — continues to emit JSON via `jq`, adapted for trigger structure
* `rbgjb/*.sh` step scripts — inlined into JSON `args` arrays at inscribe time
* All `_RBGY_*` substitution variables — baked into JSON `substitutions` object
* OCI Layout Bridge pattern — unchanged

==== Provenance Note

SLSA v1.0 provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` which will contain `cloudbuild.json`.
This is a cosmetic difference from `cloudbuild.yaml` — the structured provenance fields
(build inputs, parameters, outputs) are identical regardless of config file format.

==== References

* Research conducted: 2026-02-24 (web search + API documentation review)
* Google docs consistently present YAML examples by convention, not by requirement
* The `gcloud builds submit` CLI internally converts any config format to JSON for API submission

content.text:
**Yes — AXLA has an inline top-level JSON voicing pattern, distinct from both deflist-terms and subdocuments.**

From AXLA's form-expectation table (lines 10–12, 493–498):

| Motif family | Record form | Member form |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| `axj_structure` / `axj_field` | `mcm_form_section` | `mcm_form_bullet` under parent structure |
| `axr_record` / `axr_member` | `mcm_form_section` for record | `mcm_form_deflist` for member |
| Most other motifs | `mcm_form_deflist` | — |

So AXLA already has **three definition-site shapes** for quoins:

1. **Deflist entry** — `term::` with a short definition (most quoins)
2. **Section with structured content** — `=== heading` where the section IS the definition (JSON structures, records)
3. **Subdocument via detail-site hierarchy markers** — `include::` with `axh*_` scaffolding (regimes, operations, protocols, entities)

**The section pattern is what you're asking about.** For JSON specifically, the recipe is:

```asciidoc
=== Detection Return Structure

[[apcswr_detection_return]]
//axl_voices axj_root_structure
{apcswr_detection_return}

The top-level envelope for detection transaction returns.

* [[apcswr_return_index]]
//axl_voices axj_field axt_number axd_required
tag `index` — echoes {apcswt_transaction_index}.

* [[apcswr_return_stanford]]
//axl_voices axj_field axj_nested_field axd_required
tag `stanford` — see {apcswr_stanford_container}.

* [[apcswr_return_spacy]]
//axl_voices axj_field axj_nested_field axd_required
tag `spacy` — see {apcswr_spacy_container}.

* ...
```

- Parent quoin voiced `axj_root_structure` (or `axj_structure` for nested ones) occupies a section
- Each field is its own sub-quoin voiced `axj_field`, rendered as a bullet
- Tag strings (`index`, `stanford`) are inline literals, not quoins
- Nesting is expressed by a field pointing at another structure's anchor — `apcswr_stanford_container` gets its own section

**For APCK's three-level container output** (envelope → per-discerner wrapper → finding array), that's three sibling sections inside an APCS0 "Wire Payload Schemas" top-level section:

- `apcswr_detection_return` — root envelope
- `apcswr_stanford_container` / `apcswr_spacy_container` / `apcswr_stanza_container` — per-discerner wrappers
- `apcswr_finding_stanford` / `apcswr_finding_spacy` / `apcswr_finding_stanza` — individual finding records (spaCy/Stanza voiced `axt_variant` to cover token + entity)
- `apcswr_model_status` — readiness-probe payload

All stays in APCS0. No subdocument needed. The reach is global (container sources, app consumes, combining references, anonymization references) and the section-per-structure inline pattern scales cleanly to that — each record has its own addressable anchor, yet no external file.

**The subdocument path I was proposing earlier is overkill.** The `axj_*` inline section pattern is the right-sized tool for payload schemas. That was genuinely the gap in my thinking — I reached for the hierarchy-marker subdocument family because that's what we just built for protocol, not because records need it. The two reach levels you named map cleanly to two existing AXLA patterns: section voicing for "ambient top-level records" and hierarchy-marker subdocuments for "bounded hierarchical compositions."

Does this match what you had in mind? If so, the next-pace scope reshapes: no new AXLA family, just a new APCS0 "Wire Payload Schemas" section using `axj_*` voicings, and retire APCPS's JSON examples.

content.text:
**Sized the reference JSON.** Here's what the container actually ships:

**Structure inventory** — 7 record shapes + 1 readiness shape = **8 structures**:

| Record | Fields | Complication |
|--------|--------|--------------|
| root envelope | 4 (index, stanford, spacy, stanza) | trivial |
| `stanford` wrapper | 1 (findings[]) | trivial |
| `spacy` wrapper | 1 (findings[]) | trivial |
| `stanza` wrapper | 1 (findings[]) | trivial |
| Stanford finding | 5 (text, start, end, label, confidence) | closed 7-label enum |
| spaCy finding | **variant** on `kind` | 10 fields (token) / 5 fields (entity) |
| Stanza finding | **variant** on `kind` | 11 fields (token) / 5 fields (entity) |
| model_status (probe) | speculative | per-discerner flags + diagnostic |

**What the data reveals:**

- **Shallow nesting, rich leaves.** Only 3 levels deep (root → container → finding[]), but the per-discerner findings carry dense NLP signal.
- **Closed discriminant** (`kind`) on spaCy/Stanza — `axt_variant` with `token`/`entity` branches. Each branch has disjoint fields except for the common `text`/`start`/`end`/`kind`.
- **Field-name divergence across libraries is preserved** — spaCy uses `pos`/`tag`/`dep`; Stanza uses `upos`/`xpos`/`deprel` plus `ner`. Not normalized, and the memo argues that's load-bearing (provenance matters for combining).
- **Three distinct enum flavors:**
  - Stanford `label` — closed 7 values (`ID|HOSPITAL|PATIENT|DATE|HCW|VENDOR|PHONE`) — stable, enumerable as quoins
  - Stanza entity `label` — ~10-value OntoNotes subset (`CARDINAL|PERSON|ORG|DATE|QUANTITY|GPE|FAC|TIME|PRODUCT|PERCENT` in observed data) — large, cite taxonomy rather than enumerate
  - Stanza token `ner` — **compound BIOES encoding** (~30 observed values like `O`, `B-PERSON`, `I-DATE`, `S-CARDINAL`, `E-GPE`) — prefix × label cartesian product, describe scheme rather than enumerate
- **Two opaque-string fields** — `morph` (spaCy: `Degree=Pos`) and `feats` (Stanza: `Number=Sing`) carry their library's native morphological-feature format. Voice as `axt_string` with a format note; don't try to type the internal grammar.
- **One intra-record index reference** — `head` in token variants points at another token's zero-based position in the same findings array. AXLA has no native "sibling index" motif; voice as `axt_number` with a prose note about the semantics.
- **Stanford `confidence`** — observed 0.937–1.000 across both fixtures; voice as `axt_number` with 0.0–1.0 range, note the empirical floor.

**Schema sophistication assessment**: **moderate, not exotic.** The JSON is:

1. Wider than deep (few nesting levels, many fields per record)
2. Variant-heavy on the container findings but with a clean discriminant
3. Enum-diverse (three styles from closed-small to open-compound)
4. Straightforward on primitives (strings, ints, one float)

AXLA's existing vocabulary covers this without strain:
- `axj_structure` / `axj_root_structure` — 8 sections
- `axj_field` — ~30 field bullets (some shared field names across records get separate sub-quoins; spaCy's `text` ≠ Stanza's `text` in the quoin graph even though the tag string is the same)
- `axt_variant` with motif voicing on the finding quoins — 2 records
- `axt_enumeration` + `axt_enum_value` quoins — for Stanford's closed label set and the `kind` discriminant
- `axt_string`, `axt_number`, `axt_boolean` — primitives
- `axd_required` dimensions on every field (everything observed is populated; no optionality in current fixtures)

**No new AXLA infrastructure required** — we don't need the `axhc*_` record-hierarchy family I was pitching earlier. Section voicing + `axt_variant` covers the whole thing.

**Work size estimate for the next pace** (writing the spec, not the Rust impl):

- **~40 new quoins** in APCS0: 8 structure quoins + ~28 field sub-quoins + ~4 enum quoins (kind values, Stanford label, maybe placement-per-finding marker)
- **~500 lines of AsciiDoc** in a new APCS0 "Wire Payload Schemas" section (comparable to three-to-four regime subdocs)
- **One mapping-section revision** — promote `apcsw_` sub-letter `r` from "prose-only scaffolding" to primary (for the `apcswr_*` quoins), or park records under a different domain letter
- **Retire content from APCPS** — the illustrative-JSON section becomes obsolete; APCPS loses ~100 lines

**Character**: sonnet-scale throughout with 2-3 opus-adjacent decisions:
1. Enumerate OntoNotes labels as quoins, or cite the taxonomy and voice the field `axt_string`? (I lean cite)
2. Model Stanza's `ner` BIOES as a single `axt_string` with format note, or introduce a `axt_enumeration` naming the 5 prefixes × independent label motif? (I lean single string with note — BIOES is a scheme, not a payload-level concern)
3. Do `spacy`/`stanza`/`stanford` wrapper objects get their own `apcswr_*_container` quoins, or are they absorbed into the root envelope's field descriptions? (Absorption is lighter; separate quoins enable cross-referencing from combining rules — lean separate)

**Sizing it against existing paces**: comparable to the wire-protocol-spec pace that just wrapped (₢A9AAd) — similar length, similar complexity, one-step-removed from the same design conversation. Fits in a single pace; wouldn't need to split.

Does this match your read on sophistication? Any of those opus-adjacent decisions you want to settle now versus defer to the pace itself?

message.content: Whoa, that is more substantial than I expected.  I'm musing we might be able to 'no-op' some amount of the outputs of each container json for our purposes.  Thoughts?

content.text:
**Yes, substantial no-op room.** Three tiers depending on how aggressive:

**Tier 1 — obvious zero-value** (easy cuts, high confidence):

- **spaCy entity variant entirely.** Memo says: always labeled `ENTITY`, weight-zero. The entire `kind=entity` branch on spaCy is 283 findings per fixture that carry no signal. Drop from schema; prose-only mention.
- **Stanza token `ner` field** (BIOES tags like `B-PERSON`/`I-DATE`). The Stanza entity variant is *derived* from these — so the `ner` field on tokens is redundant with `apcswr_finding_stanza_entity`. Keep the entity variant, drop `ner` from the token variant.
- **Opaque-string fields** (`morph`/`feats` in library-native format). Prose-only acknowledgment: "present on wire; Clipbuddy ignores."

That drops ~8 quoins and eliminates one variant, with effectively zero cost to combining (memo already confirms these are unused signals).

**Tier 2 — redundancy-based** (medium confidence, requires a decision):

- **One parser's syntactic signals are redundant with the other's.** spaCy and Stanza both provide POS + dependency + lemma on tokens. The memo argued two-parser corroboration was load-bearing, but ₢A9AAV hasn't written any rule using spaCy-AND-Stanza-agreement yet — it's aspirational.
  - **Keep spaCy tokens, drop Stanza tokens entirely** — loses second-parser corroboration, saves ~11 fields × 1000+ tokens per fixture.
  - **Keep both but slim each** — formalize only the 3 fields combining actually names (spaCy `pos` for AUX/PROPN split; Stanza entity `label` for Stanford corroboration) and prose-acknowledge the rest.

**Tier 3 — aggressive minimum** (only what combining demonstrably uses):

Per APCS0's own load-bearing declarations:
- Stanford: the whole finding (5 fields)
- spaCy: POS tags only ("may/May/may disambiguation relies on POS tags")
- Stanza: the entity-variant findings (second-source NER corroboration)

That collapses to **3 finding shapes, ~15 quoins** — down from ~40. Spec becomes ~200 lines instead of ~500.

**My take** — Tier 1 is almost-free; take it. Tier 2 hinges on whether the aspirational "two parsers corroborating" stays aspirational or becomes an actual combining rule. If ₢A9AAV ends up writing a rule like "spaCy-and-Stanza both say PROPN → strong proper-noun signal," we need both libraries' POS fields. If combining only uses spaCy POS + Stanza entity labels, Stanza tokens become load-free.

**Key lever**: the schema can be **tighter than the wire**. Spec only what combining will reference as named identities; prose-acknowledge what the container sends but algorithm ignores. Container Python code stays unchanged — this is a schema trimming, not a wire change. We pay for fields we formalize (quoin count, spec prose), not for fields the container happens to emit.

**Additional rationale for aggression**: combining (₢A9AAV) is where these fields earn their quoin status by being referenced in rules. Writing the full schema now means minting quoins we *might* not end up needing. Tier 3 forces the "prove it's load-bearing" discipline at spec time. If ₢A9AAV later needs `deprel` for a rule, adding a quoin is cheap; removing one that never got used is awkward.

Want to commit to Tier 1 + some Tier 2 (pick one parser's tokens), and let ₢A9AAV's concrete rules drive any additions? Or is there value in keeping the schema faithful to everything the container emits, even unused fields?

    with open(f'/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Memos/memo-20260420-apck-container-fixture-assay/{fname}') as f:
        d = json.load(f)

    entities = [f_ for f_ in d['spacy']['findings'] if f_.get('kind') == 'entity']
    print(f"spaCy entities: {len(entities)}")

    labels = Counter(e['label'] for e in entities)
    print(f"Distinct labels: {dict(labels)}")

    # Sample 20 entity texts across the doc
    print(f"\nFirst 30 entity texts (position-ordered):")
    for e in entities[:30]:
        print(f"  [{e['start']:5d}-{e['end']:5d}] {e['text']!r} (label={e['label']})")

    # What fraction of the doc do entities cover?
    total_char_coverage = sum(e['end'] - e['start'] for e in entities)
    print(f"\nTotal char coverage: {total_char_coverage}")

    # Are there overlaps with Stanford findings?
    stanford_spans = [(f_['start'], f_['end'], f_['label'], f_['text']) for f_ in d['stanford']['findings']]
    spacy_entity_spans = [(e['start'], e['end'], e['text']) for e in entities]

    # Check: for each Stanford finding, does ANY spacy entity cover that exact span or overlap?
    exact_match = 0
    overlap_only = 0
    no_coverage = 0
    for s_start, s_end, s_label, s_text in stanford_spans:
        exact = any(es == s_start and ee == s_end for es, ee, _ in spacy_entity_spans)
        overlap = any(not (ee <= s_start or es >= s_end) for es, ee, _ in spacy_entity_spans)
        if exact: exact_match += 1
        elif overlap: overlap_only += 1
        else: no_coverage += 1
    print(f"\nStanford vs spaCy-entity overlap:")
    print(f"  Exact span match: {exact_match}/{len(stanford_spans)}")
    print(f"  Partial overlap only: {overlap_only}/{len(stanford_spans)}")
    print(f"  No spacy-entity coverage: {no_coverage}/{len(stanford_spans)}")

    # How do spacy entities differ from stanza entities?
    stanza_entities = [f_ for f_ in d['stanza']['findings'] if f_.get('kind') == 'entity']
    stanza_entity_spans = [(e['start'], e['end'], e['label'], e['text']) for e in stanza_entities]

    # How many spacy entities have no stanza-entity counterpart?
    only_spacy = 0
    for es, ee, etext in spacy_entity_spans:
        has_stanza = any(not (ze <= es or zs >= ee) for zs, ze, _, _ in stanza_entity_spans)
        if not has_stanza:
            only_spacy += 1
    print(f"\nspaCy entities with no Stanza-entity overlap: {only_spacy}/{len(spacy_entity_spans)}")
CONTENT
